title: The Practice and Theory of Bolshevism
description: The Practice and Theory of Bolshevism — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: "Table of Contents"
memes.description: "Table of Contents for The Practice and Theory of Bolshevism"

memes.label: Allied Blockade
memes.description: The economic blockade of Soviet Russia by the Allied Powers after World War I — analysed by Bertrand Russell as a policy that inflicted immense suffering on the Russian civilian population while failing to achieve its political objective of weakening Bolshevik rule.

memes.label: Allied Intervention in Russia
memes.description: The military intervention by Britain, France, the United States, Japan, and other Allied Powers in the Russian Civil War (1918–1920) — condemned by Bertrand Russell as a policy that prolonged the conflict, deepened Russian suffering, and strengthened the Bolsheviks' domestic position.
aliases: foreign intervention in Russian Civil War

memes.label: America
memes.description: The United States as it figured in Bertrand Russell's analysis of international responses to Bolshevism — a rising power whose President Woodrow Wilson embodied a liberal internationalism that Russell found well-intentioned but ineffective in addressing the realities of postwar Europe and revolutionary Russia.

memes.label: Anarchism
memes.description: The political philosophy advocating the abolition of all forms of coercive authority including the state — discussed by Bertrand Russell in relation to Bolshevism as a revolutionary tradition that shared Bolshevism's critique of capitalism but rejected its authoritarian methods and statism.

memes.label: Art
memes.description: Bertrand Russell's analysis of art under Bolshevism — the tension between artistic freedom and propaganda, the divorce between art and popular feeling, and the fate of theatre, crafts, and mass pageantry in revolutionary Russia.

memes.label: Congress of the Peoples of the East
memes.description: "The 1920 Baku Congress of the Peoples of the East, convened by the Comintern to mobilise anti-colonial movements across Asia — a pivotal moment in Bolshevik international strategy that Russell analysed as exemplifying the dual character of Soviet policy: genuine anti-imperialism combined with instrumental manipulation of national movements."
aliases: First Congress of the Peoples of the East

memes.label: Arthur Balfour
memes.description: Foreign Secretary:A senior British statesman involved in the Allied response to the Russian Revolution — representing, in Russell's context, the conservative establishment's hostility to Bolshevism.

memes.label: Bavaria
memes.description: Bavaria, site of the short-lived Bavarian Soviet Republic of 1919 — a revolutionary episode that Russell cited as illustrating both the international resonance of Bolshevism and the fate of communist uprisings in societies lacking the conditions that had permitted Bolshevik success in Russia.

memes.label: Béla Kun
memes.description: Béla Kun (1886–1938), Hungarian communist revolutionary who led the Hungarian Soviet Republic of 1919 — the most significant attempt to replicate Bolshevik revolution outside Russia, whose rapid failure Russell cited as evidence of the limits of Bolshevik revolutionary strategy.

memes.label: Bolshevik Party
memes.description: The revolutionary Marxist political party led by Vladimir Lenin that seized power in the October Revolution of 1917 and governed Soviet Russia, analyzed by Bertrand Russell as combining religious zeal with authoritarian practice.

memes.label: Bolshevik Propaganda
memes.description: Bertrand Russell's analysis of Bolshevik propaganda as a central instrument of governance and ideological control in Soviet Russia, combining mass persuasion with state power to an extent unprecedented in modern history.

memes.label: Bolshevism
memes.description: The political theory and practice of the Bolshevik Party as analyzed and critiqued by Bertrand Russell in 1920, combining elements of religion, revolutionary hope, and authoritarian governance.

memes.label: "Bolshevism: Theory versus Practice"
memes.description: A synthetic analysis of Bertrand Russell's central critique in The Practice and Theory of Bolshevism — the widening gap between Bolshevik theoretical ideals and the practical realities of Soviet rule as Russell observed them in 1920.

memes.label: Bourgeoisie
memes.description: The capitalist class that owns the means of production — a central concept in Marxian theory and a primary target of Bolshevik policy, analysed by Bertrand Russell in the context of its systematic disenfranchisement and suppression in Soviet Russia.

memes.label: Britain
memes.description: The leading Allied Power whose Russia policy Bertrand Russell — a British subject writing for a British audience — analysed most extensively, criticising both the Churchillian push for intervention and the hypocrisy of British moral condemnations of Bolshevism.

memes.label: Capitalism
memes.description: The economic system based on private ownership of the means of production, which the Bolsheviks sought to abolish and which Russell analyzed as both exploitative and, in certain respects, less oppressive than Bolshevik methods.

memes.label: Cheka
memes.description: The Cheka (All-Russian Extraordinary Commission for Combating Counter-Revolution and Sabotage) — the Bolshevik secret police established in December 1917, which Russell identified as the institutional embodiment of Bolshevik terror and a disturbing continuity with Tsarist methods of political repression.

memes.label: Georgy Chicherin
memes.description: Bolshevik People's Commissar for Foreign Affairs, cited by Bertrand Russell as an example of how Bolshevik class theory classified non-proletarian leaders as members of the proletariat based on ideological correctness.

memes.label: China
memes.description: China, referenced by Russell as a potential target of Bolshevik anti-colonial outreach and as an example of the non-European societies to which Bolshevik doctrines of world revolution were expected to appeal.

memes.label: Winston Churchill
memes.description: British Secretary of State for War (1919–1921), cited by Russell as a leading advocate of Allied anti-Bolshevik intervention whose attitude was predictable under Marxian theory.

memes.label: Class Struggle
memes.description: The Marxian doctrine that history is driven by conflict between economic classes, adopted by the Bolsheviks as a justification for revolutionary violence and dictatorship.

memes.label: Communism
memes.description: A classless, stateless social and economic system based on common ownership of the means of production, as advocated by Marx and pursued by the Bolsheviks.

memes.label: Communist Party (Bolsheviks)
memes.description: The ruling political party of Soviet Russia, numbering about 600,000 members, which Russell describes as the sole repository of political power under the Bolshevik regime — analogous to the guardians in Plato's Republic.

memes.label: Constituent Assembly
memes.description: The All-Russian Constituent Assembly — the democratically elected body convened in January 1918 and dissolved by the Bolsheviks after a single day, an event Russell cited as the definitive moment when Bolshevik commitment to democracy gave way to one-party dictatorship.

memes.label: Cooperative Movement
memes.description: The system of voluntary cooperative associations for production and consumption — representing, for Bertrand Russell and other democratic socialists, a decentralised alternative to Bolshevik state ownership that preserved individual initiative while advancing collective welfare.

memes.label: Democracy
memes.description: The system of government by popular consent and majority rule — analysed by Bertrand Russell as the standard against which Bolshevik one-party rule must be measured, and as a political value that the Bolsheviks rejected both in theory and in practice.

memes.label: Anton Denikin
memes.description: Anton Denikin (1872–1947), Tsarist general and leader of the White Army in South Russia during the Russian Civil War, whose military campaign threatened Bolshevik control of the Donets Basin and whose scorched-earth retreat Russell cited as evidence of the Civil War's destructiveness.

memes.label: Dictatorship
memes.description: The Marxist concept of the dictatorship of the proletariat — the transitional state form between capitalism and communism — which Russell analysed as the theoretical justification for Bolshevik one-party rule and the key site where Bolshevik theory diverged most sharply from democratic socialism.

memes.label: Dictatorship of the Proletariat
memes.description: The transitional state form in Marxist theory through which the working class was to exercise power after revolution; Russell's critique focused on how it functioned as minority rule in Bolshevik practice.

memes.label: Education
memes.description: Bertrand Russell's analysis of education under Bolshevism — the concentration on the rising generation, the tension between Communist indoctrination and free intellectual development, and the relationship between education and industrial needs in revolutionary Russia.

memes.label: England
memes.description: England as Russell's primary comparative reference point throughout his analysis of Bolshevism, representing the advanced industrial democracy whose political conditions made violent revolution both unlikely and inappropriate.

memes.label: Entente (Allied Powers)
memes.description: The coalition of capitalist powers — primarily Britain, France, and the United States — whose blockade and military intervention against Soviet Russia Russell condemned as both hostile and counterproductive.

memes.label: Fall of Tsarism
memes.description: The February Revolution of 1917 that ended three centuries of Romanov rule in Russia — the political event that created the conditions for the Bolshevik seizure of power later that year, analysed by Bertrand Russell as the collapse of an autocracy that had lost all legitimacy.

memes.label: Family under Bolshevism
memes.description: Bertrand Russell's observations on the Bolshevik transformation of marriage and family law — one of the most radical experiments in social legislation in modern history, abolishing traditional legal structures while confronting the material realities that limited genuine emancipation.

memes.label: Finland
memes.description: Finland, which fought a brutal civil war in 1918 between socialist Reds and conservative Whites shortly after declaring independence from Russia — a conflict Russell referenced as part of the wider pattern of revolutionary turmoil that the Bolshevik Revolution unleashed across the former Russian Empire.

memes.label: France
memes.description: The major Allied Power whose hostility to Bolshevism Russell documented — driven by French investment losses in Russia and the strategic imperative of containing revolutionary contagion on the European continent.

memes.label: French Revolution
memes.description: The French Revolution (1789–1799) — a pivotal event in modern European history that Russell and the Bolsheviks both referenced as a historical precursor and point of comparison for the Russian Revolution.

memes.label: Friedrich Engels
memes.description: "Friedrich Engels (1820–1895), co-founder with Karl Marx of scientific socialism and author of Socialism: Utopian and Scientific — whose materialist philosophy Russell analysed as the intellectual foundation of Bolshevik orthodoxy and whose transformation of Marxian theory into a closed philosophical system Russell regarded as a key source of Bolshevik dogmatism."

memes.label: Georges Clemenceau
memes.description: French Prime Minister (1917–1920), cited by Russell as one of the Entente leaders whose hostility to Bolshevism was predictable under Marxist theory.

memes.label: German Revolution of 1918–1919
memes.description: The revolutionary upheaval that overthrew the German Kaiser and established the Weimar Republic — watched intensely by the Bolsheviks as the first great test of whether revolution would spread from Russia to an advanced industrial country.

memes.label: Germany
memes.description: The major European power whose military defeat in World War I and subsequent revolution created the international context for Bolshevik survival — analysed by Bertrand Russell as the country where Bolshevik-style revolution was most anticipated and most feared.

memes.label: Maxim Gorky
memes.description: Russian writer and political activist, sympathetic to the Bolshevik cause but critical of its excesses, who worked to preserve Russian intellectual and artistic life during the early Soviet period.

memes.label: Grain Requisitioning
memes.description: The Bolshevik policy of forcibly seizing grain from the peasantry to feed the cities and the Red Army — analysed by Bertrand Russell as the central economic policy of War Communism and the primary source of conflict between the Bolshevik state and the Russian peasantry.

memes.label: Guild Socialism
memes.description: Guild Socialism — the British movement advocating workers' control through national industrial guilds — which Russell personally favoured as a democratic alternative to both state socialism and Bolshevik dictatorship, combining industrial democracy with functional representation.

memes.label: Arthur Henderson
memes.description: British Labour Party leader and a key figure of the Second International, whom Lenin cited as an example of the futility of parliamentary socialism.

memes.label: Hungary
memes.description: Hungary, site of the Hungarian Soviet Republic of 1919 — a 133-day Bolshevik-inspired revolutionary government led by Béla Kun that Russell analysed as the most significant European attempt to replicate the Russian Revolution outside Russia, and whose rapid collapse illustrated the fragility of revolutionary regimes lacking broad popular support.

memes.label: Imperialism
memes.description: Imperialism as analysed by Russell in relation to Bolshevism — both as the capitalist world-system the Bolsheviks sought to overthrow, and as a tendency Russell feared Bolshevism itself might reproduce in the form of a Napoleonic authoritarian empire.

relations.label: Baku Congress of the Peoples of the East

memes.label: Index of Concepts
memes.description: Query-based index of all concept entities in the wiki, sorted alphabetically by label.

memes.label: Index of Organizations
memes.description: Alphabetical index of all organization pages in the wiki — political parties, international bodies, military formations, and other organised groups referenced in Russell's The Practice and Theory of Bolshevism.

memes.label: India
memes.description: India, the largest British colony, referenced by Russell as a key example of imperial exploitation and as a target of Bolshevik anti-colonial strategy following the 1920 Baku Congress.

memes.label: Industrial Conscription
memes.description: The Bolshevik policy of compulsory labour service and military-style organization of industrial workers, implemented during the Russian Civil War period.

memes.label: Industrialism
memes.description: The system of machine-based industrial production that, in Russell's analysis, is both a material force shaping social consciousness and a potential threat to human values under Bolshevism; central to Marxian historical materialism and to Russia's electrification programme.

memes.label: Intelligentsia
memes.description: The educated intellectual class of Russia — analysed by Bertrand Russell as a social group caught between the Bolshevik regime's need for their expertise and its suspicion of their independence, with many forced to choose between silent cooperation and persecution.

memes.label: Internationalism
memes.description: Bertrand Russell's analysis of Bolshevik internationalism — the Third International, the commitment to world revolution, and the tension between Lenin's universalist vision and the resurgence of nationalist sentiment in revolutionary Russia.

memes.label: Jean Jaurès
memes.description: French socialist leader and anti-militarist, a leading figure of the Second International, assassinated in 1914 on the eve of the First World War.

memes.label: Lev Kamenev
memes.description: Bolshevik leader and President of the Moscow Soviet, whom Bertrand Russell met and stayed with during his 1920 visit to Russia, learning about Soviet governance from him.

memes.label: Karl Marx
memes.description: Karl Marx (1818–1883), German philosopher, economist, and revolutionary theorist, whose materialist conception of history and theory of class struggle formed the intellectual foundation of Bolshevism, and whose doctrines Russell subjected to sustained philosophical critique.

memes.label: Karl Kautsky
memes.description: Karl Kautsky (1854–1938), the leading theoretician of German Social Democracy and orthodox Marxism, whose critique of Bolshevik dictatorship — particularly his 1918 pamphlet The Dictatorship of the Proletariat — Russell drew upon to argue that Leninism represented a fundamental break with, rather than a continuation of, the Marxist tradition.

memes.label: Alexander Kerensky
memes.description: Alexander Kerensky (1881–1970), head of the Russian Provisional Government overthrown by the Bolsheviks in October 1917, whom Russell cited as a benchmark of governance — noting that industry under the Bolsheviks was "much worse than even under Kerensky."

memes.label: Alexander Kolchak
memes.description: Admiral of the White Army who led anti-Bolshevik forces in Siberia during the Russian Civil War, mentioned by Bertrand Russell as evidence of the brutal fighting the Bolshevik regime faced.

memes.label: Leonid Krasin
memes.description: A Bolshevik diplomat and trade negotiator who represented Soviet Russia in negotiations with Britain — embodying, for Bertrand Russell, the Bolsheviks' capacity for pragmatic engagement with the capitalist world despite their revolutionary rhetoric.

memes.label: Kronstadt Rebellion
memes.description: The March 1921 Kronstadt Rebellion — an armed uprising of sailors and soldiers at the Kronstadt naval base demanding soviet democracy without Bolshevik party dictatorship, whose brutal suppression Russell cited as definitive evidence that Bolshevik rule had become a one-party tyranny fundamentally hostile to workers' self-determination.

memes.label: Labour Desertion
memes.description: The widespread phenomenon in 1920 Soviet Russia of workers leaving their assigned employment in search of food or speculative gain — a crisis that Russell documents through official Communist reports, revealing the coercive measures the Bolshevik state adopted in response.

memes.label: Law and Justice under Bolshevism
memes.description: Bertrand Russell's analysis of the transformation of law and justice under Bolshevism — the replacement of Tsarist legal institutions with revolutionary tribunals guided by class considerations rather than formal legal principles, and the consequences of subordinating law to political objectives.

memes.label: Vladimir Lenin
memes.description: Vladimir Lenin (1870–1924), the founding leader of Soviet Russia and the Bolshevik Party, whose one-party dictatorship, Marxian orthodoxy, and revolutionary tactics Russell examined critically after an hour's personal conversation in Moscow.

memes.label: Liberty
memes.description: Bertrand Russell's defence of individual liberty as an intrinsic political good — argued against the Bolshevik view that freedom was a "bourgeois prejudice" to be sacrificed for economic equality, forming the philosophical core of his critique of Bolshevism.

memes.label: David Lloyd George
memes.description: The British Prime Minister (1916–1922) whose pragmatic approach to Soviet Russia — supporting limited trade relations while resisting full-scale intervention — represented, in Bertrand Russell's analysis, a more realistic alternative to the Churchillian push for military confrontation.

memes.label: London
memes.description: London — the capital of the British Empire, referenced by Russell as a point of contrast with Moscow in his analysis of the geopolitical and cultural distances separating Bolshevik Russia from the Western democracies.

memes.label: Anatoly Lunacharsky
memes.description: Bolshevik Commissar for Education, cited by Russell in his analysis of Soviet education policy and the tension between humanistic ideals and industrial demands.

memes.label: Marxian Orthodoxy
memes.description: Bertrand Russell's analysis of the transformation of Marxism from a scientific hypothesis into an infallible doctrine under Bolshevism — the epistemological shift that, Russell argued, was the essential tragedy of the Russian Revolution.

memes.label: Marxism
memes.description: The political and economic theories of Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels, forming the ideological foundation of Bolshevism — subjected by Bertrand Russell to sustained critical examination focusing on the transformation of Marxian theory from scientific hypothesis into infallible religious dogma under Bolshevik rule.

memes.label: Materialism
memes.description: The philosophical doctrine — adopted by Marx and central to Bolshevik ideology — that material and economic conditions fundamentally determine social, political, and intellectual life; analyzed and critiqued by Russell as a dogmatic element of Bolshevism.

memes.label: Materialistic Conception of History
memes.description: The Marxian theory that economic causes are the fundamental driving force of historical and political change — a doctrine Russell subjects to sustained philosophical critique in Part II of his work, distinguishing it sharply from philosophic materialism.

memes.label: Mensheviks
memes.description: The moderate faction of the Russian Social Democratic Labour Party that opposed Lenin's Bolsheviks — advocating a more gradualist, democratic path to socialism and eventually suppressed by the Bolshevik regime they had once debated as equals.

memes.label: Moscow
memes.description: Capital of Soviet Russia and seat of the Bolshevik government, observed by Russell during his 1920 visit as the centre of Communist political power, the headquarters of the Third International, and a city whose daily life embodied the contradictions of the Bolshevik experiment.

memes.label: Nationalization
memes.description: The Bolshevik policy of transferring private property — land, factories, banks, and housing — into state ownership, analysed by Bertrand Russell as both a fulfilment of socialist principle and a source of economic disruption that contributed to Russia's industrial collapse.

memes.label: October Revolution
memes.description: The Bolshevik-led insurrection of November 1917 (October in the Russian calendar) that overthrew the Provisional Government and established Soviet rule — the decisive rupture Russell analyses as inaugurating an unprecedented but tragically flawed political experiment.

memes.label: Paris Commune
memes.description: The revolutionary workers' government that ruled Paris from March to May 1871, celebrated by Marx as the first example of the dictatorship of the proletariat.

memes.label: Peasantry
memes.description: The rural agricultural class that constituted over eighty percent of Russia's population — analysed by Bertrand Russell as the decisive social force whose relationship with Bolshevism was marked by deep ambivalence and whose material interests conflicted fundamentally with Communist economic policy.

memes.label: Person Index
memes.description: Query-based index of all person entities in the wiki, sorted alphabetically by label.

memes.label: Petrograd
memes.description: Former imperial capital and Russell's first destination in Soviet Russia, noted for the striking pageant of the World Commune at the Stock Exchange and the contrast between revolutionary zeal and monumental beauty.

memes.label: Philosophic Materialism
memes.description: The philosophical doctrine, central to Marxist theory, that matter is the fundamental substance in nature and that mental and social phenomena are ultimately determined by material conditions.

memes.label: Plato's Republic
memes.description: Plato's Republic — the classical Greek philosophical text on justice and the ideal state, invoked by Russell as an ironic comparison to Bolshevik claims of representing the dictatorship of the proletariat; Russell argued that Bolshevik rule more closely resembled the rule of Platonic guardians than genuine democracy.

memes.label: Poland
memes.description: The newly independent state whose war with Soviet Russia in 1920 formed the immediate military context of Bertrand Russell's visit — a conflict that tested Bolshevik military capacity and exposed the tension between Soviet Russia's internationalist rhetoric and its defence of national territory.

memes.label: Political Opposition under Bolshevism
memes.description: The systematic suppression of political opposition under Bolshevik rule — which Russell identified as the defining feature distinguishing Bolshevik practice from democratic socialism, and the central reason his initial sympathy for Soviet Russia turned to critical opposition.

memes.label: Press and Censorship
memes.description: Bertrand Russell's analysis of the Bolshevik state's monopoly over the press and public expression — a system of information control that suppressed dissent, eliminated independent journalism, and transformed all media into instruments of Party propaganda.

memes.label: Proletarian Art
memes.description: The Bolshevik cultural policy of fostering a new, revolutionary art expressive of proletarian values — a policy Russell observed with cautious sympathy in 1920 Russia, noting both its admirable ambition and its practical difficulties.

memes.label: Proletariat
memes.description: The industrial working class, which Marxist theory identifies as the revolutionary agent destined to overthrow capitalism and establish the dictatorship of the proletariat.

memes.label: Rationing under Bolshevism
memes.description: The system of state-controlled distribution of food and consumer goods in Bolshevik Russia — observed by Bertrand Russell as a central feature of daily life in Moscow, where ration cards determined survival and the black market filled the gaps the state could not.

memes.label: Red Army
memes.description: The military force of Soviet Russia, created by Trotsky in 1918, which Russell observed as both the defender of the revolution and an instrument of domestic coercion including food requisitioning.

memes.label: Red Terror
memes.description: The campaign of political repression and violence conducted by the Bolshevik regime during the Russian Civil War — analysed by Bertrand Russell as a practice that, while partly a response to civil war conditions, reflected a deeper Bolshevik hostility to opposition as a matter of theoretical principle.

memes.label: Religion
memes.description: Bertrand Russell's analysis of Bolshevism as a religion — a set of dogmatic beliefs held beyond evidence — and the Bolsheviks' Marxist attitude toward traditional religion, explored as a central theme in The Practice and Theory of Bolshevism.

memes.label: Representative Government
memes.description: The system of governance through elected representatives, which Russell contrasts with Bolshevik dictatorship — arguing that while Parliamentarism has flaws, the Bolshevik rejection of constitutional methods carries graver dangers.

memes.label: Revolution
memes.description: A fundamental transformation of social, economic, and political structures, central to Marxist theory and exemplified by the Bolshevik Revolution of 1917.

memes.label: Bertrand Russell
memes.description: Bertrand Russell (1872–1970), British philosopher, logician, and social critic, author of The Practice and Theory of Bolshevism (1920) based on his firsthand visit to Soviet Russia in 1920.

memes.label: Russia
memes.description: The country whose Bolshevik revolution and post-war condition Russell examined during his 1920 visit — a vast, peasant-majority nation of 120 million undergoing an unprecedented social and political transformation that Russell assessed with a mixture of admiration, sympathy, and sharp critique.

memes.label: Russian Civil War
memes.description: The multi-sided armed conflict from 1917 to 1922 in which the Bolshevik Red Army fought against the White armies and foreign intervention forces.

memes.label: Russian Revolution
memes.description: The series of revolutions in Russia during 1917 that overthrew the Tsarist autocracy and brought the Bolsheviks to power — a "great heroic event" Russell compares to the French Revolution and the rise of Islam in its world-historical significance.

memes.label: Scientific Socialism
memes.description: The Marxian claim that socialism is not a utopian aspiration but a scientifically demonstrated necessity of historical development — analysed by Bertrand Russell as a powerful but dangerous idea whose transformation from hypothesis to dogma was central to the Bolshevik tragedy.

memes.label: Second International
memes.description: The international organisation of socialist and labour parties founded in 1889 — whose collapse in 1914 in the face of World War I Russell identified as the event that discredited moderate socialism and opened space for Bolshevik radicalism.

memes.label: Siberia
memes.description: The vast eastern territory of Russia that served as a theatre of civil war, foreign intervention, and the traditional place of Tsarist exile — discussed by Bertrand Russell in the context of Allied military operations and the geographical scale of the Russian catastrophe.

memes.label: Socialism
memes.description: An economic and political system based on collective or state ownership of the means of production, critically examined by Bertrand Russell in relation to Bolshevik methods and Western alternatives.

memes.label: Soviet
memes.description: The councils of workers, peasants, and soldiers that formed the basic political structure of revolutionary Russia, analysed by Bertrand Russell as the institutional foundation of Bolshevik rule — combining genuine grassroots origins with increasing Party control.

memes.label: Soviet Constitution
memes.description: The constitutional framework of the Soviet Republic as described and critiqued by Bertrand Russell in 1920, characterized by a formal representative structure that in practice concentrated all power in the Communist Party.

memes.label: Yakov Sverdlov
memes.description: Bolshevik party organizer and chairman of the All-Russian Central Executive Committee, whom Russell met aboard a Volga steamer in 1920.

memes.label: Syndicalism
memes.description: The revolutionary trade union movement advocating workers' control of industry through direct action — discussed by Bertrand Russell as a tradition that shared Bolshevism's revolutionary commitment but offered a more democratic vision of post-revolutionary society based on workers' self-management rather than Party dictatorship.

memes.label: Red Terror
memes.description: The Red Terror — the Bolshevik campaign of political repression and mass violence during the Russian Civil War, which Russell identified as an integral feature of Bolshevik rule rather than a temporary defensive measure.

memes.label: Third International (Comintern)
memes.description: The international communist organization founded in 1919 to promote world revolution and coordinate communist parties globally, which Russell analyzed as the vehicle of Bolshevik revolutionary strategy.

memes.label: Trade Unions
memes.description: Labour organizations that Russell discusses in both their Soviet context — where they were subordinated to state economic mobilization — and the British context, where he argued they were a preferable alternative to revolutionary violence.

memes.label: Treaty of Brest-Litovsk
memes.description: The peace treaty signed between Soviet Russia and the Central Powers in March 1918, ending Russia's participation in World War I at the cost of enormous territorial concessions — a diplomatic episode that revealed the tension between Bolshevik internationalist ideology and the imperatives of state survival.

memes.label: Leon Trotsky
memes.description: Leon Trotsky (1879–1940), Bolshevik revolutionary, Red Army organizer, and Lenin's most prominent colleague, whom Russell found to possess "lightning intelligence and magnetic personality," though his vanity and Napoleonic ambition gave cause for concern.

memes.label: Tsarism
memes.description: The autocratic system of government under the Romanov dynasty that ruled Russia until the February Revolution of 1917 — analysed by Bertrand Russell as the oppressive predecessor against which Bolshevism defined itself and whose methods of secret police and censorship the Bolsheviks ultimately reproduced.

memes.label: Vanguard Party
memes.description: The Leninist concept of the vanguard party — a disciplined organisation of professional revolutionaries claiming to represent the "class-conscious" proletariat — which Russell identified as the organisational key to Bolshevik power and the structural basis of one-party dictatorship.

memes.label: Treaty of Versailles
memes.description: The peace settlement that ended World War I — condemned by Bertrand Russell as a punitive peace that perpetuated the injustices of the old diplomacy and created conditions conducive to both Bolshevism and right-wing reaction across Europe.

memes.label: Volga
memes.description: Major Russian river along which Russell travelled from Nizhny Novgorod to Saratov, observing peasant life, regional conditions, and the transport and fuel economy of Soviet Russia.

memes.label: War Communism
memes.description: The economic policy of the Bolshevik regime during the Russian Civil War (1918–1921) — characterised by grain requisitioning, nationalisation of industry, and the abolition of market exchange, analysed by Bertrand Russell as both a response to emergency conditions and a reflection of Bolshevik economic ideology.

memes.label: White Army
memes.description: The White Army — the counter-revolutionary military forces that fought the Bolsheviks during the Russian Civil War under generals including Denikin, Kolchak, and Yudenich, whose reliance on foreign support and association with the old regime Russell noted as factors contributing both to their military effectiveness and their ultimate political failure.

memes.label: Women under Bolshevism
memes.description: Bertrand Russell's observations on the condition of women in Bolshevik Russia — noting progressive reforms in marriage law and education alongside the material hardships that fell disproportionately on women during the Civil War period.

memes.label: Woodrow Wilson
memes.description: 28th President of the United States (1913–1921), cited by Russell as an example of democratic leaders who won elections on one platform and governed on another.

memes.label: World Revolution
memes.description: The Bolshevik doctrine that communism can only be permanently established through simultaneous or successive revolutions in all advanced capitalist countries, critically examined by Bertrand Russell.

memes.label: World War I
memes.description: The global military conflict from 1914 to 1918 that devastated Europe and created the conditions for the Russian Revolution.

memes.label: Nikolai Yudenich
memes.description: White Army general who led the Northwestern Army against Petrograd during the Russian Civil War, mentioned by Bertrand Russell in the context of Bolshevik propaganda art.
